---
title: "Two Questions to Answer Before You Choose an Analysis"
subtitle: "Poster Project &middot; Applied Statistics"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-copy: true
    embed-resources: true
---

Your project must include inference --- confidence intervals and hypothesis
tests --- not just description. But inference only means something when you can
say *who or what you are inferring about* and your variables fit the tools we
have. Work through both questions below with your group, and be ready to defend
your answers at the poster plan meeting.

## Question 1: What population does your inference describe?

Look at your data and ask: what does one row represent, and how did the rows
get into this dataset? You are probably in one of three situations.

-   **A sample from something larger.** The rows were sampled from a bigger
    group (2,500 of 32,000 marathon finishers). Your CI and test have their
    usual meaning --- but state the population precisely. Is it all
    marathoners, or only runners fast enough to qualify for Boston?

-   **A self-selected or filtered group.** Rows had to opt in or pass a cutoff
    to appear (salaries people chose to report; games that sold at least
    100,000 copies). Inference is still legitimate, but the population is
    narrower than the topic suggests. Name the group your data can actually
    speak for, and say who is missing.

-   **Essentially the whole population.** You have every US county or every
    country. There is no larger group to generalize to, so what would a
    confidence interval even estimate? Here a randomization test is often the
    more meaningful tool: it asks whether the association you see is stronger
    than what random chance alone would produce. If you use one this way,
    interpret it that way --- not as a statement about sampling.

Whichever situation you are in, your Methodology section should say so in one
or two honest sentences. Vague claims ("we can generalize to all movies") will
cost you more than a candid limitation will.

## Question 2: Do your variables fit the tools we have?

Our inference procedures compare *two* means or *two* proportions, or examine
one mean, one proportion, or one correlation. Many categorical variables have
five, ten, or thirty categories. You cannot feed a 12-level variable into a
two-group comparison --- but you can reshape the question. Legitimate moves (R
templates for each are in the next section):

-   **Choose two groups to compare** and subset to them. Pick the pair for a
    substantive reason, decided *before* you look at the results --- not
    because they gave the most exciting difference.

-   **Collapse categories into two meaningful ones.** One level vs. all others
    ("Nintendo vs. other publishers"), or a sensible grouping ("Europe vs.
    elsewhere"). Define the grouping in your methodology.

-   **Create a binary variable.** From a category ("is the genre Action?") or
    from a quantitative variable ("profitable: gross exceeds budget?"). A
    defensible cutoff, stated in advance, turns many questions into a
    proportion.

**One warning:** do not run comparisons for every possible pair of categories
and report the one with the smallest p-value. Testing many pairs practically
guarantees that something looks "significant" by chance alone. Choose your
comparison first; then test it.

## R templates: reshaping a variable for two-group inference

Replace the `CAPITALIZED` placeholders with names from your own dataset. Keep
the quotation marks.

After `library(mosaic)`, the verbs `filter()` and `mutate()` below are ready to
use. Each template changes your data and saves the result with `<-` ; nothing
changes unless you save it. Category names inside quotes must match your data
exactly --- spelling, capitals, and spaces. When in doubt, run
`tally(~ CATVAR, data = MYDATA)` first to see the exact names and counts.

### 1 &middot; Keep only two groups

``` r
MYDATA2 <- MYDATA |>
  filter(CATVAR %in% c("Level A", "Level B"))
```

``` r
games2 <- video_games |>
  filter(genre %in% c("Action", "Sports"))
```

*Example: keep only Action and Sports games, saved as a new dataset `games2`.*

### 2 &middot; One category vs. everything else

``` r
MYDATA <- MYDATA |>
  mutate(NEWVAR = ifelse(CATVAR == "Level A", "Level A", "Other"))
```

``` r
video_games <- video_games |>
  mutate(maker = ifelse(publisher == "Nintendo", "Nintendo", "Other"))
```

*Example: a new variable `maker` with exactly two values, Nintendo and Other.*

### 3 &middot; Collapse several categories into two groups

``` r
MYDATA <- MYDATA |>
  mutate(NEWVAR = ifelse(CATVAR %in% c("Level A", "Level B"),
                         "Group 1", "Group 2"))
```

``` r
hollywood <- hollywood |>
  mutate(audience = ifelse(rating %in% c("G", "PG"),
                           "Family", "Mature"))
```

*Example: G and PG films become Family; PG-13 and R become Mature.*

### 4 &middot; Binary variable from a quantitative variable

``` r
MYDATA <- MYDATA |>
  mutate(NEWVAR = ifelse(QUANTVAR > CUTOFF, "High", "Low"))
```

``` r
hollywood <- hollywood |>
  mutate(profitable = ifelse(worldwide_gross > budget, "Yes", "No"))
```

*Example: the cutoff can be another variable, as here, or a number you chose in
advance.*

### Check your work, then use the new variable

``` r
tally(~ NEWVAR, data = MYDATA)
```

The tally should show exactly two groups with sensible counts --- if you see a
third group or a count of zero, a name inside your quotes doesn't match the
data. Once it checks out, use the new variable anywhere the original would go:
in `gf_boxplot()`, in StatKey, or in a two-group interval or test. Remember to
state in your methodology how and why you defined the grouping.

## Before the poster plan meeting

For each inference you plan: (1) name the population or chance process it
describes, (2) name the parameter (a mean? a difference in proportions? a
correlation?), and (3) explain how you reduced any many-category variable to a
form our tools handle. If you can answer all three, you have an analysis plan.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #595959;"}
E. Nordmoe &middot; Math 260 &middot; Kalamazoo College
:::
